---
title: "Intent-to-Execution Contracts"
chapter_id: "intent-to-execution-contracts"
part_id: "planning-memory-reasoning-execution"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-27"
primary_sources:
  - "viea"
  - "talos"
  - "software_magic_grimoire"
  - "genesiscode"
  - "moecot"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Four local raw-cache mappings are passage-reviewed for `viea`, `talos`, `software_magic_grimoire`, and `genesiscode`; `moecot` remains connector/source-note mapped without usable local raw text."
  - "Only protocol schema/fixture validation is implemented; no behavioral execution tests have been run for this chapter."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `intent-to-execution-contracts` |
| Part | Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-27 |
| Primary source records | `viea`, `talos`, `software_magic_grimoire`, `genesiscode`, `moecot` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `viea`; supporting: `talos`, `software_magic_grimoire`, `genesiscode`; connector/recovery: `moecot` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `viea`, `talos`, `software_magic_grimoire`, `genesiscode`, `moecot`; passage-reviewed local raw cache: `viea`, `talos`, `software_magic_grimoire`, `genesiscode`; connector/recovery without local raw passage review: `moecot` |
| Test state | `intent_contract.valid.json` and `intent_execution_trace.valid.json` pass protocol fixture validation; `AsiStackProofs.IntentToExecution` implements finite constraint-preservation and missing-approval running gates; behavioral execution, replay, and artifact-satisfaction tests remain planned. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter treats execution as a governed trace, not as a successful response. The repository contains draft schemas and fixtures for the trace shape, but it does not yet contain a deployed intent-to-execution runtime, a replayed vertical slice, or empirical evidence that a contract was satisfied.

It opens Part II by turning Part I's governance boundary into an operational spine. The system can now ask not only whether a request is admissible, but how that request becomes planned, authorized, executed, verified, and fed back without losing its original constraints.

The contract is an authority-preserving lease over work. It says what the system is allowed to try, which means are forbidden, what result would count as satisfaction, which side effects require approval, and when the system must come back to the human or governance layer instead of improvising.

::: {.asi-human-only}
## Human Reading Path

Part I defined the boundaries that keep capability governed. Part II begins by asking how a real request moves through those boundaries without turning into an untraceable conversation. The answer is an intent-to-execution contract: a path from purpose to plan, artifact, authority check, execution, verification, feedback, and residual. The contract is useful only if another component, reviewer, or future run can inspect it without guessing what the system thought it was allowed to do.

The book now shifts from principles to machinery. A useful AI system should not merely respond well. It should be able to show what request it understood, what work it created, what powers it used, what evidence it produced, and what remains unresolved. That trace is not paperwork for its own sake. It is the control surface that lets planning, tool use, memory, approval, rollback, and later learning stay attached to the original intent instead of drifting into improvisation. The contract keeps that trace anchored when later systems optimize around it.
:::

## Problem

Human goals need a typed path into execution that preserves requirements, artifacts, verification, deployment, and feedback.

Part I produced governed intent: purpose, authority ceilings, protected invariants, rights, and self-improvement boundaries. Part II begins when that intent must become work. VIEA's central move is that intent should not jump straight to a model response or a tool call. It should become a chain of typed artifacts: intent contract, command contract, plan, typed job, runtime action, artifact, verification record, feedback, and residuals.

The bridge between governance and cognition is the intent-to-execution contract. Planning, memory, routing, execution, and evidence can only do disciplined work if the request they receive has a durable identity, constraints, acceptance conditions, side-effect limits, and an artifact trace.

The bridge has to separate desire, authorization, means, and acceptance. A human may want an outcome without authorizing every path to that outcome. A plan may find a clever means that violates the contract. An artifact may be useful without satisfying the acceptance predicate. Treating those as separate fields keeps execution from expanding beyond intent.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

A model response is not an execution contract; it lacks lifecycle, acceptance, artifact identity, and side-effect control.

The common failure is to treat fluent text as completed work. A response can sound responsive while hiding missing approvals, untracked source boundaries, unverifiable artifacts, unexecuted tests, or side effects that were never authorized. Talos sharpens the point: work should enter a job lifecycle, not a loose conversational state. GenesisCode adds the software version of the same boundary: proposals, effects, replay logs, and obligations must be separated.

The stack needs a contract boundary because later layers should not infer authority from prose. A planner can decompose a contract. A runtime can enforce a contract. A verifier can check a contract. A bare response cannot reliably do any of those things.

The missing-field case is just as important as the filled-field case. If the contract does not specify authority, acceptance, allowed tools, failure behavior, or approval path, downstream layers should not silently infer permissive defaults. They should emit clarification requests, draft-only routes, residuals, or blocked states.

## Core Claim

[intent-to-execution-contracts.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] The stack should transform intent into execution contracts before any tool or runtime action occurs.

The claim remains at `argument` support. VIEA supplies the intent-to-artifact spine, Talos supplies the typed-job and audit pressure, Software Magic Grimoire supplies the command-envelope vocabulary, GenesisCode supplies effect/provenance discipline, and MoECOT is still treated as an implementation-reference route rather than local proof.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C now maps this core claim to all assigned source notes, and four local raw-cache mappings have been passage-reviewed. Those reviewed passages support the need for an intent-to-execution contract boundary before tool or runtime action, but they do not prove a deployed runtime, trace replay, artifact satisfaction, or behavioral execution tests. `moecot` remains a connector/source-note mapping until public-safe runtime artifacts or usable raw text are available.

| Source | What it supports | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| `viea` | Passage-reviewed: intent becomes structured command contracts, durable artifacts, routed work, verified outputs, runtime execution, feedback, residuals, tools, benchmarks, and regression coverage. | Architecture proposal only; no deployed runtime, replayed vertical slice, or artifact-satisfaction test is proven here. |
| `talos` | Passage-reviewed: work lowers into typed job lifecycles with decomposition, contract locks, execution, adjudication, delivery, feedback, audit, replay, approvals, and residual feedback. | Security, approval behavior, benchmark claims, and tool-effect enforcement are not reproduced or verified in this repository. |
| `software_magic_grimoire` | Passage-reviewed: bounded operational instructions with roles, objectives, context, constraints, procedure, output, verification, failure behavior, handoff artifacts, guards, and recovery paths. | Promptcraft vocabulary only; no empirical prompt-performance result, execution runtime, or behavioral test is present. |
| `genesiscode` | Passage-reviewed: AI proposals remain separate from execution through deterministic kernels, capability-mediated effects, semantic patches, provenance hashes, replay logs, obligations, and protocol seals. | No prototype, replay checker, proof artifact, benchmark, or security audit is present here. |
| `moecot` | Runtime-reference context for compact orchestration, specialist lanes, fail-closed ledgers, readiness gates, replay, residual tracking, and handoff. | Connector-readable context only; code, logs, release artifacts, and benchmarks have not been imported or reproduced. |

## Mechanism

VIEA supplies the spine: intent becomes command contracts, durable artifacts, routed work, runtime execution, verification, feedback, and residuals. Talos adds the job-lifecycle pressure that makes work auditable and replayable. Software Magic Grimoire supplies the bounded instruction envelope, GenesisCode keeps proposals and effects behind provenance and obligation boundaries, and MoECOT supplies runtime-reference ledgers, readiness gates, replay, and handoff language without becoming local runtime proof.

The passage-reviewed sources sharpen the boundary rather than relaxing it. They support the contract shape, trace vocabulary, artifact discipline, and effect/provenance interface. They do not establish that a contract compiler exists, that side effects were enforced, that a runtime adapter ran, or that an artifact satisfied its intent. Those stronger claims require a replayed vertical slice with command, environment, artifact, verification, approval, and residual records.

An Intent-to-Execution Trace is the record that lets those pieces hand off without treating a fluent response as completed work.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Human intent"] --> B["Intent contract"]
  B --> C["Command contract"]
  C --> D["Plan graph"]
  D --> E["Typed jobs"]
  E --> F{"Authority + approval gate"}
  F -- "allow" --> G["Runtime adapter / tool"]
  F -- "deny or missing" --> H["Residual / escalation"]
  G --> I["Artifact"]
  I --> J["Verification record"]
  J --> K["Feedback + evidence ledger"]
  H --> K
```

The trace represents command contracts with inputs, constraints, artifacts, tools, evidence, approvals, and feedback. Each transition routes the contract through the layer that actually owns the next responsibility: planning decomposes, memory supplies bounded context, verification checks acceptance, execution causes side effects only under authority, and evidence attaches the result back to claims, regressions, and residuals.

The trace is append-only at the level that matters: each transition either produces the next artifact or records why it could not. If a contract cannot be planned, that is a residual. If a job cannot run because approval is missing, that is a residual. If an artifact is produced but verification fails, that is a residual with a repair path.

This is why the book treats feedback as part of execution, not as a postscript. Result artifacts should update claims, regression suites, tools, and future plans. Failures should become residuals, not disappear into the next prompt.

The trace should also preserve re-contract points. If planning discovers that the original objective is underspecified, the required tool exceeds authority, the context is inadequate, the verifier cannot check the output, or the cost exceeds budget, the correct move is not creative completion. It is re-contract, escalation, deferral, or refusal.

The updated public record shape makes that refusal path explicit. Intent contracts now carry an intake state, bounded defaults, re-contract triggers, and non-claims. The execution trace carries handoff receipts, dispatch receipts, re-contract events, stop or fault state, and non-claims. Those fields do not make the trace executable. They make it harder for a future implementation, reviewer, or chapter-writing run to confuse "the record was shaped correctly" with "the work was authorized, run, verified, or accepted."

## Interfaces

The interface is the Intent-to-Execution Trace.

- Intent contracts feed planning.
- Execution consumes typed jobs derived from contracts.
- Evidence records contract satisfaction.

Minimum interface fields span the parent intent contract and the execution trace:

- `trace_id`
- `intent_contract`
- `intake_state`
- `command_contract`
- `plan_graph`
- `typed_jobs`
- `authority_transitions`
- `handoff_receipts`
- `dispatch_receipts`
- `artifacts`
- `verification_records`
- `delivery_or_deployment`
- `feedback_refs`
- `recontract_events`
- `stop_or_fault_state`
- `residuals`
- `bounded_defaults`
- `recontract_triggers`
- `non_claims`

Intent contracts feed planning. Command contracts bind procedure and outputs. Plan graphs decompose the work. Typed jobs carry executable units. Authority transitions control side effects. Verification records decide whether the artifact satisfies the original intent.

The interface should record authority deltas at every lowering step. Intent may authorize a goal; command contracts narrow procedure; plans allocate tasks; jobs request tools; runtime adapters ask for effects. Each step can preserve, narrow, expire, deny, or require approval. It cannot silently widen.

## Invariants

- Contract constraints survive compilation.
- Side effects require explicit execution authority.
- Artifacts remain linked to source intent.

The key invariant is preservation across compilation: the plan, job, artifact, and verification record must still be traceable to the parent intent. If the system cannot show that lineage, it has not executed the intent; it has only produced something near it.

A second invariant is acceptance separability. Producing an artifact, running a tool, or satisfying a subtask is not the same as satisfying the parent intent. The trace must keep local completion separate from contract satisfaction.

Residual obligations also survive acceptance, so later repair cannot treat partial success as closed intent.

Contract closure requires an explicit acceptance state, not merely the absence of an error.

## Failure modes

- Response mistaken for completed work.
- Artifact identity lost.
- Approval bypass.

The first failure is especially dangerous for a living book: a chapter can be drafted without the evidence matrix, source trace, or validation state being updated. Artifact identity loss breaks audit and repair. Approval bypass turns planning into execution authority. Each failure should block promotion until the trace is repaired.

The subtle failure is helpful overrun. The system goes beyond the contract because it predicts that the human would approve, then records the extra work as initiative. Governed execution should treat unapproved expansion as a new contract request, not as success.

Another failure is stale acceptance, where an old approval is reused after the artifact, tool, or risk tier changed.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

A command-contract schema and one end-to-end intent-to-artifact trace.

The intent-to-execution path starts with an `intent_contract` schema, a `command_contract` schema, and an `intent_execution_trace` fixture that links an intent contract, plan graph reference, typed job, authority transition, handoff receipt, dispatch receipt, artifact, verification record, re-contract event, stop or fault state, and residuals. Validation confirms trace shape only; whether the work satisfied the intent remains unverified.

Those records are implemented only as public protocol artifacts. They can validate that the trace names the required links and non-claims; they cannot show that the links were executed, authorized, accepted, or useful.

A two-path vertical-slice fixture would make the contract chain visible: one contract that lowers into a permitted job and accepted artifact, and one contract that stops because authority, context, approval, verifier, or budget is missing. The slice would exercise preservation and stop conditions while leaving behavioral execution unclaimed.

## Beyond the State of the Art

Intent-to-execution contracts mature into the operating spine for the whole stack, not a larger form of the minimal fixture. Every serious request would enter as a durable intake object, receive an explicit authority envelope, lower through command, plan, job, artifact, verification, delivery, feedback, and residual records, and remain inspectable after the work is done.

Once contracts become the execution spine, no response is allowed to masquerade as execution. A human, reviewer, runtime, or later AI agent can ask what was requested, what was authorized, which defaults were used, what re-contract events occurred, which jobs ran, which side effects were approved, which artifacts were produced, which validators accepted them, and which residuals remain. The system can pause or stop for missing context, missing approval, missing verifier, budget exhaustion, or violated constraints without treating that stop as conversational failure.

The final product surface would look like a work ledger with contract intelligence:

- Intake contracts preserve user purpose, constraints, rights, authority basis, bounded defaults, stop conditions, and non-claims.
- Lowering receipts prove that command contracts, plan nodes, typed jobs, runtime calls, artifacts, and verification records still point back to the same parent intent.
- Runtime gates consume explicit authority transitions rather than inferring permission from helpful prose.
- Re-contract events are first-class records, so discovered scope changes, approval gaps, verifier gaps, or impossible budgets become new decisions instead of hidden improvisation.
- Feedback closes into claims, regressions, tools, source notes, and residual ledgers, which lets future work reuse what was learned without inventing a false success record.

This governed transaction protocol remains a target architecture, not a current-result claim. Support should stay at `argument` until contract schemas, parent-child trace checks, approval gates, replayed execution records, acceptance receipts, and review logs show that a useful result was also contract-linked, authority-preserving, and accepted under the stated criteria.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Intent and trace fixture validation | Check that the intent and trace fixtures match the public schemas and preserve intake state, handoff receipts, dispatch receipts, re-contract triggers, stop/fault state, residuals, and non-claims. | implemented by protocol validation; validated locally |
| Contract field completeness test | Check that required objective, constraints, outputs, verification, approvals, and failure behavior are present. | planned; not run |
| Constraint preservation test | Check that constraints in the intent contract remain visible in command, plan, job, and artifact records. | planned; not run |
| Artifact traceability test | Check that produced artifacts point back to intent, command, verification, and residual records. | planned; not run |

Only the fixture-shape check is implemented. The remaining tests require either an executable vertical slice or a replayed trace with command, environment, artifact, verification, and residual records.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:intent_execution.contracts.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.IntentToExecution` | A compiled execution job preserves the parent contract constraints. | implemented |
| `lean:intent_execution.contracts.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.IntentToExecution` | An execution job without required approval cannot transition to running. | implemented |

These Lean hooks are implemented as finite-record predicates over execution compilation and approval reviews. They prove only the record-level gates: a compiled job preserves declared parent constraints when the review says preservation is valid, and a job missing required approval cannot transition to running. They do not prove a deployed runtime, trace replay, tool-effect enforcement, artifact satisfaction, or end-to-end execution correctness.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `viea` | Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture | whole_stack_execution_spine | Keystone source. Human intent -> command contracts -> artifacts -> routing -> runtime targets -> verification -> deployment -> feedback. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `talos` | Talos Protocol | labor_execution_os | AI labor OS. Deterministic cognitive manufacturing, typed jobs, control planes, auditability, tool isolation. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `software_magic_grimoire` | Software Magic Grimoire | command_contracts_promptcraft | Structured command contracts and semantic density of software words. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `genesiscode` | GenesisCode | executable_specification | Tiny pure calculus + obligations + provenance for auditable AI-symbiotic programming. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `moecot` | MoECOT-Agent Architecture Whitepaper | implementation_reference | Concrete implementation evidence: governed low-parameter multi-core runtime, readiness gates, ledgers, replay. | source note available; connector or recovery required |

The assigned sources establish the trace vocabulary for intent, plan, job, artifact, verification, feedback, and residual links. Four local source mappings now have passage review, but the core claim stays at `argument` until a local vertical slice records execution evidence or another accepted evidence transition justifies narrower promotion.

## Summary

Intent-to-execution contracts are the first operational handoff. They turn human intent into a traceable chain of contracts, plans, jobs, artifacts, verification, feedback, and residuals.

The point is not bureaucracy. It is continuity: requirements, authority, artifacts, and evidence have to survive the trip from purpose to action. That trace narrows into the command language that makes the first machine-readable handoff precise.

A downstream layer should never have to guess whether it is allowed to act. If it has to guess, the trace should stop or re-contract. This is also where the human-facing promise becomes concrete. A person should be able to see how a request was interpreted, which constraints traveled with it, which authority was granted, which assumptions were introduced, and which residuals remained unresolved. That visibility makes later automation less mysterious and more accountable. Planning can decompose the work, memory can supply context, and tools can execute only because the contract gives them a bounded object to inherit rather than an ambient desire to satisfy.
